seoTitle: How to Create Articles in Zendesk Guide
metaDescription: Learn how to create articles in Zendesk Guide, from drafting with the rich text composer to organizing sections, tags, and viewer permissions.
intro: Explaining the same issue in ticket after ticket wastes agent time that a self-service article could save. Zendesk Guide's content management panel lets you draft, format, and publish help documents customers can find on their own. This guide walks through the full publishing workflow.

steps.heading: Open the article canvas
steps.body: Click the top global navigation Add button and select Article to open a blank text editor.

steps.heading: Write the title and body
steps.body: Input a direct, search-optimized title and compose your content using the rich text formatting toolbar.

steps.heading: Add media and tables
steps.body: Use the media tools to insert brand images, video frames, and tables where they clarify the steps.

steps.heading: Use the HTML editor for complex layouts
steps.body: Switch to source code mode if you need to drop in custom style scripts or responsive external frames.

steps.heading: Set section and author placement
steps.body: In the right sidebar, select the section placement and assign author ownership for the article.

steps.heading: Control visibility and publish
steps.body: Configure visibility toggles to separate public viewers from internal agents, add SEO tags, and click Publish.

faq.question: Can I restrict an article to internal agents only?
faq.answer: Yes, the visibility toggle lets you isolate internal documentation from public search directories.

faq.question: Do I need coding knowledge to add tables or images?
faq.answer: No, the standard rich text toolbar handles tables and images without needing code, though HTML mode is available for advanced layouts.

faq.question: Can multiple articles link to each other?
faq.answer: Yes, you can add hyperlinks between articles within the rich text editor.

faq.question: Why should I add search tags to an article?
faq.answer: Tags improve how easily the article surfaces in internal knowledge base search and self-service matching.
